parsing_res_list.block_content: Inthe faceof decliningrevenuesand risinginput prices,manyunivrsities are looking at ways to reduce operating costs. The two biggest cost itemsforuniversitiesarepayrollandpnsionsliabiitandthecostf maintaiinandptampubilivrte to address how to make cost savings in these two areas.

Getting visibility into key cost drivers is achallenge, leading many institutionstoengageitactialcostcutingexercissatherthanworkng totransformtheicosttructues.omeofthesetactiltitivede cuttingravlb,illndd consumables byrevisitingprocurementproceses,witchingsuppliersr renegotiatingcontracts.

However, this approach was aptly described by a few university leaders as merely "trimming around the margins". To make a real impact on operating costs, tougher decisions will need to be made, including investing in technology, scaling back or cutting non-core projects, services or support.A few of the universities interviewed had already undergone mergers to achieve economies of scale and reduce duplication.

When considering the best cost reduction strategies, most universities will need to improve their visibility of cost drivers, and in particular to understand which costs are fixed, and which are variable. To streamline cost structures, institutions require access to more granular and up-to-date data on the true costs of running each course and maintaining each center.

Leaders are reluctant to include job reductions in cost-cutting measures,but some have been forced to reduce headcount, tenured status and salary costs to lower expenditure. Other approaches to reducing payroll include cutting non-teaching staff through hiring freezes,replacing leavers with more junior staff and merging roles.

However, these initiatives will only get institutions so far. Some universities are thinking more strategically about their workforce strategies to prevent over-hiring, using data to predict demand and hire accordingly.

While headcount reductions save money in the short term, leaders are apprehensive about the longer-term costs associated with these measures.Some noted the cultural cost of redundancies, with a loss of morale as remaining staff fear for the safety of their jobs. One Vice Chancellor in Australiaadatducafumbrivablmoved wo had just beentrainedorupskilld,cratingerorturonthisinvestmen.Leaders are alsoconcernedthat reducing headcountcouldeode the capacity of staff to engage in essentialtransformation projects.

Our biggest balance sheet risk is using our reserves for current operational expenses.and with our revenue going down, not generating enough profi t to add to our reserves.
CFO, Public University, US
We invest a lot of money and time in developing people,so to get rid of them only to re-hire them in 12 months doesn't make sense.
Professor Patricia M. Davidson Vice Chancellor and President of the University of Wollongong,Australia
